title: Non-modal Scutes Patterns in the Green Turtle Hatchlings: a Possible Effect of Clutch Mass on the Frequency.
authors: Bektaş Sönmez, Cemil Aymak, Aşkın H Uçar, Remziye S Ergene Üzdiyen
summary: Researchers studied green sea turtle hatchlings to understand why some develop unusual shell patterns (non-modal scute patterns) instead of the typical pattern. They found that the total weight of eggs in a nest appears to influence how often these unusual patterns occur, accounting for about 26% of the variation, though other environmental factors likely play a role too.
key_points: About 25% of the 1,064 hatchlings studied had non-standard shell scute patterns instead of the typical arrangement
Clutch mass (total egg weight) was the strongest factor linked to unusual pattern frequency, more so than nest depth or sand coverage
Multiple factors—both genetic and environmental—likely work together to cause these pattern variations, but clutch mass alone doesn't fully explain them
why_it_matters: Understanding what causes variation in sea turtle development helps scientists monitor population health and may reveal how environmental conditions affect embryonic development in these endangered animals.
